T: Tell the Story
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved. 	1
Oren Klaff: 	I’m Oren Klaff, and we’re here in Pitch Mastery talking about Pitch Anything and
the STRONG Method. So we did S in STRONG, which was Set the Frame. And
now we’re on to T, which is Tell the Story. Then after this, we’ll be in R: Reveal
the Intrigue, O: Offer the Prize, N: Nail the Hook Point, and G: Get the Deal
Strong.
So I think it’s very important, and this is maybe the most understood part of
pitching or giving presentations is the story. Everyone’s focused on “tell the
story.” And let’s maybe drop back and think about story a little bit, from our
ancestors’ perspective. Our ancestors evolved to live in groups, hunter-gatherers.
And they had to make sense of increasingly complex social relationships. They
started living in communities, and living in a community requires keeping tabs on
who the group members are and what they’re doing and how they relate. And this
is why storytelling developed. This is how we developed the skill of storytelling.
And to this day, we still act like hunter-gatherer people in a way, in that we spend
most of our conversations telling personal stories and gossiping. And even CEOs
of hundred-million-dollar companies spend much of their time in what scientists
would call gossip. Why? Because stories do a great job of conveying emotion
and getting people to empathize with you and consider your—as we talked about
in Frames, your point of view.
A story gives people the opportunity to experience something to same way that
you experienced it. That’s very helpful. And this is probably why I hear people
give the advice, “When you sell, tell a story. When you make a presentation, tell
a story. When you pitch your deal to investors, right, make sure to tell a story.”
Really most of the business stories I hear are not good. I hear a lot. I hear them
every day. Pitches. They’re not good, and in fact really most are bad. They’re
really bad because they’re put together for the wrong purpose. Most things that
people think are stories are just actually well-formed arguments. A couple little
interesting tidbits. They’re not stories.
So if you’re going to a business meeting, yes, you do need a story. And in fact
my own method, STRONG—the T stands for Tell the Story—but I’m here to tell
you why and what kind of story you actually need to tell. Clear up a lot of
misconceptions. And really, since we’re here working on pitch mastery, which is
really the pro-level stuff, let’s call a story what it is—a narrative. That’s what
scientists call it, what business people call it, it’s what ad agencies and venture
capitalists and the guys who really do business core to the movement of money in
the economy. They call that a narrative, business narrative.
There are a couple reasons why you tell a story, and it’s not what you think. It’s
not so people understand your product, and it’s not so people like you, and it’s not
to build emotional rapport. There are much more effective ways to do these
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 1 of 17 --

T: Tell the Story
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved. 	2
things. We talked about many of them in Framing. In a business setting, a
narrative has four purposes. We’ll talk about it right now.
Number one, narrative transport, narrative transport. The reason I will tell a story
in a business meeting is that somebody else can repeat that story when I’m gone.
That’s the job of a story, might be the number-one job. Nobody doing business
deals, or in large economic transactions, is hardly ever able to make a decision on
their own. They have to hear what you’re talking about and go tell it to other
people.
They live in an ecosystem. They have accountants and lawyers, and they might
tell it to their wife or their family. Some of the biggest deals are killed because
people go home, and at the end of the day they can’t explain what it is to their
wife. And their wife goes, “That doesn’t make any sense to me.” And the
business guy gets kind of shocked back into a different state of reality, and he
goes, “Yeah, you know, it doesn’t make sense. If I can’t explain it to you, why
should I be able to do this deal?” The business story has to have narrative
transport. You tell it to someone, and they have to be able to go tell it to someone
else.
I’ll give you an example, a deal I’m working on. So we are flying our corporate
jet into San Diego Airport, and the pilot—there’s three of us on board—and the
pilot comes on and says, “Guys, sorry, we have to divert to Palomar Airport,
which is 45 miles to the north.” So we say, “Why? Is there weather?” He says,
“No, they’re busy. We couldn’t get tower clearance.”
What does that mean, they’re busy? Well, San Diego International Airport is the
busiest single runway airport in the world, even busier than Gatwick Airport in
London. They’re at capacity. And what they will do is, they would rather land a
plane with 170 people on it than a plane with three people on it, so charter and
private jets they send up north.
So we spent $8,000 more for the hour it takes to circle around and get a landing
pattern at Palomar Airport, and we land and we’re an hour away from where we
actually want to be. So we’ve spent $8,000 more and we’re an hour away from
our meeting, and the day is ruined.
Now, you or anyone can repeat that throughout an ecosystem. “Hey, you know,
San Diego Airport is at capacity. These guys got rerouted, it cost them $8,000,
they end up 45 miles away. It’s repeatable and understandable, and it has
narrative transport.
The second reason you would tell a story—again, not to build rapport and all that
stuff—to anchor your situation, your business situation, your proposition in
reality, with what I call psychological realism. Everyone around us has a natural
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 2 of 17 --

T: Tell the Story
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved. 	3
lie detector for psychological realism. We can tell when something rings false. I
give you an easy example. The lion woke up in her cave and said, “I’m hungry.”
She heard her cubs going meow, which means I’m hungry too. And she looked
out of the cave and in the distance saw a zebra. She got up on her haunches and
shook her head and screamed off across the plain. Chased the zebra, chased it
around for an hour, couldn’t catch it. Tired. Rest a little bit, look around.
Sees an antelope, knows antelope are slower than zebra at this time of the day.
[deep breath] Restored. She chases after the antelope, jumps on it. The antelope
puts up a fight and claws dig in. She drags it to the ground and kills it with a bite
to the neck and blood gushes out. And she drags it three-quarters of a mile back
to the cave. Little cubs go, “Great, meow,” and they start digging in and eating.
And they eat and they have their fill, and half of it’s left. And they live for a
month. The lion family lives for another 90 days in the harsh reality of the
Serengeti.
Look, I don’t know anything about lions. It’s—I made it up on the spot. Don’t
know anything about this stuff, but it feels real, doesn’t it? Because it happens
with psychological realism. For whatever reason, you know this is a true story, it
happens every day probably. We just know this stuff. So we have good lie
detectors. You’re going to anchor your deal in reality by telling a story that feels
real to people because it happens in human time scales. In human understandable
physical environments. Feels real. That’s the second reason—to make it feel
real.
So the third reason—again, we’re not looking for rapport or to have people know
that we’re good storytellers or that we’re funny or that we’re interesting or just to
fill the silence. You’re not going to do any of those things. The third reason:
we’re going to avoid putting someone else’s mind into paradigmatic mode. And
all that’s a big word. It just means cold analysis. Stories bring people over to the
narrative side of thinking. They don’t put on a cold analysis.
There’s a—you can look him up—there’s a great scientist, I’ve talked to him.
Bruner, Bruner wrote about two modes: narrative and paradigmatic. The
paradigmatic mode is the mode of science, and it’s concerned with logically
categorizing the world. Categorizing, analysis, cold stuff. The other mode—
Bruner called it the narrative mode and I call it that as well—is concerned with
building meaning and describe human experiences through stories. Stories are
human or humanlike intention and action and the consequences that mark the
course of people’s lives. They capture people’s explanations about what they
want and how they’ll go about achieving it.
So if we put people in a logical, mathematical, analytical, paradigmatic mode,
they will be grinding us. They’ll be grinding us and constantly testing the things
we’re saying for truth and falsehood and mathematical correctness. And they
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 3 of 17 --

T: Tell the Story
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved. 	4
won’t become interested in us as characters and believe that we are honest and
truthful and aspiring to bring them value. They’ll be constantly questioning,
trying to trip us up. When you put people in paradigmatic mode, the analytic
mode, you become the beta, because they will see themselves as the alpha—the
alpha doing analysis and trying to figure out if what you’re talking about is true
and real.
So each mode—paradigmatic and narrative—has significant strengths, but they
are incompatible. They’re incompatible. Paradigmatic mode, the last thing I’ll
say about it, it offers up the power of prediction, so it is useful. When you’re
going to decide how much money you’re going to make next year in your
business or your household or what it costs to go on vacation or buy a copier for
your building, you’re going to use paradigmatic mode because it sets up
hypotheses and the nature of reality and what will unfold in the future and
likelihoods and statistics and probabilities.
But in contrast, the narrative mode, the storytelling mode, organizes complexities
and ambiguities into the human world of interaction and action, puts it in a
meaningful structure that people can understand. These two modes of thought are
not complementary, and they differ radically. If you put someone in paradigmatic
mode, you’re going to have trouble influencing your deal, because they’re going
to make many of the decisions for themselves. If you put somebody in narrative
mode, they’re going to listen to you.
So there’s a fourth reason beyond this, why we’ll use a story. We may not open
up with it, but we have it in our toolkit. It’s to recapture attention when attention
dwindles. Oh, you can feel, you can feel when people are fading from you. Or
you can see it on the mouse-screen, they start to click away or they go somewhere
else. There’s a definite sense, either on technology and the Internet or in person
or on the phone, when people are fading away.
And at that time, you can introduce a story, because when you talk about humans
doing something, people become interested. When you talk about abstract
notions, people’s attentions fade very quickly. So you use it to recapture attention
when it dwindles. The way—we can talk about this later—but there’s very easy
ways to do that. You can say, “Hey, this reminds me of the time that we were
faced with a decision like you. We made the wrong decision. Really screwed us
up.” And that will, you can tell a little story about that, and people will suddenly
pay more attention.
So let’s summarize this and move into how to do it. This is the why and what it’s
used for. Next we’re going to go into how. A good story and a well-formed
argument are different things. A good story and a well-formed argument are
different things, as we’ve been saying. Both can be used as a means for
convincing. I’m not saying you abandon talking about your product in technical
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 4 of 17 --

T: Tell the Story
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved. 	5
terms or talking about the financial benefit of what you offer or selling your deal
on the merits of the finance. Those are well-formed arguments and they have
their place, and we’re going to talk about that. But a good story is different.
So arguments convince people that something is likely to happen. A well-formed
argument convinces people that something is likely to happen. Stories, narratives,
convince people that what you’re talking about is true, and they bring people in,
they bring you in. Well-formed arguments are about providing empirical proof.
A narrative establishes your truthfulness, and that’s the difference.
We’ve learned a little bit about the purpose of the story. We’re going to get real
specific here shortly. The exact steps to take, and the steps I take, that I get
narratives that go raise hundreds of millions of dollars. So I’m wearing this tie,
and I have my hair like this, and I have to wear a suit, not because I do anything
else. This is what I do, is I find narratives, I take them into boardrooms, I present
them to people who control money, and I try to get them to give me the money.
I’m not a surfer, I’m okay riding motorcycles but I don’t it very often. I don’t
really have any other skill. The only reason—I don’t go camping, tell stories
around the fireside. I don’t run a kindergarten, help little kids understand how to
deal with the world. I’m not a bartender that listens to your problems or tells you
about mine. The only reason I care about this stuff—you can make money with
it. You can make more money than the next guy who is delivering information in
a really crappy way that’s disorganized and has a disconnect with the human
condition.
So I care about this stuff and make money with it. I don’t know anything else.
You can ask me anything out of this studio, talk about anything else, and I’ll say I
don’t know anything else, but I do know how to craft narratives that make money.
So let’s go deeper into it. Now, again, people say tell your story, tell your
narrative. Why? So people understand you better. Right? And I will call you
out on that or anyone who says stories help people understand better. That is not
accurate, alright? People are really damn smart, they understand.
I’ll give you a quick example. If I say to you, for companies with large buildings
in California who are dissatisfied with their aging solar panels, my product is a
plug-and-play solar accelerator. Whoa! Provides 35 percent more energy than
old panels, and unlike the cost of getting rid of old panels, my product is plug-
and-play. You just put it on there, it’s inexpensive, and it has no moving parts.
Save 35 percent, put it on your existing solar panels, save 35 percent and earn
your money back within a year. There’s no story there. Right? You understand
that. Anybody would.
Stories, narratives don’t explain stuff, they don’t make stuff easier to understand.
Stuff is easy to understand. Products are easy to understand. “Oh, you have ball
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 5 of 17 --

T: Tell the Story
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved. 	6
bearings and they spin a little bit faster because they have a Teflon coating, and
they’re a little bit more expensive, but they last 10 times as long.” I get it!
Please, don’t wrap this in a story or a narrative. My God! You have a squirrel nut
scratcher and it’s made out of harder plastic, and it lasts four more years, and it’s
25 percent cheaper. My God, I get it! Took me four seconds to describe it.
Please, don’t put narratives around stuff that don’t need it.
Most stories are born, because they aren’t real stories—they’re time lines of
unusual events. Let me give you a good one. This Russian oligarch, Prokhorov,
owns the Nets. Know how he made his billions? He was a partner in a mineral
concern in Russian, and they owned a lot of manufacturing and mineral assets. It
was on “60 Minutes.” And so at some point, he was running around with women
and being a spectacle in the French and Swiss newspapers. And his partners came
to him and said, “Sorry, we’ve got to kick you out. You’re too high-profile. We
don’t like this P.R. we’re getting because of this, the way you’re in the papers all
the time with women and drinking and God-knows-what. So we’re kicking you
out, take your shares. We’re going to buy them back from you.” They paid him
billions for it.
And then—and this is the great part—the downturn, the financial downturn that
hit, and they all lose a huge part of their money. And Prokhorov is sitting there
with billions because he was kicked out because he ran around with women and
was drinking in Switzerland. I love it! But it’s not a damn story. Right? It’s a
time line of unusual events that are a little bit salacious and provocative. You get
a little ping of dopamine. It’s not a damn story that has any use.
So what is a story? A story is something with a character you care about, who is
in high-stakes situations, and he’s trying to rise above his circumstance and find
that fine line between good and evil and create a better world for both himself and
people around him. And there are consequences to his decisions, and none of
those decisions are easy. That’s a narrative. They do this in the “Walking Dead,”
right? And they talked about this before, they put the character in these decisions.
Should I operate on my son with a butter knife and an aspirin? Or should I walk
four miles to the abandoned hospital that’s full of zombies, flesh-eating zombies,
and try to find some penicillin and some gauze? Right? High-stakes decisions
that have life-and-death consequences. That’s embedded in a story.
Another thing that I think is good and I want to talk about this throughout
STRONG and Pitch Mastery, but quickly—story, narrative, narrative, story, puts
you on a narrative arc. You know where you are, like a game of golf, right? To
get to hole five, you have to have gotten to hole four and hole three and hole two.
And so when you’re on a narrative arc, you can take someone through
increasingly interesting circumstances and dramatic tension. And you have things
to hit and you know where you are, because as we get to later in STRONG, the
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 6 of 17 --

T: Tell the Story
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved. 	7
nail, the hook point, and get the deal, you have to know where you are along the
narrative arc. So it’s going to be important.
The way I tell a story is probably different than how you do it, alright? My story
goes like this: “Here’s the big idea. If you could do this amazing thing, right?
And you would make lots of money. But it’s not easy, and here’s why it’s not
easy, but we’re going to do it anyway. And when we do, we’re going to have a
massive success.” Right? That’s how I get into a narrative.
Let me give you an example. If you could build a small airport to take hundreds
of flights a day away from San Diego Airport, which is one of the busiest airports
in the world, then you would have an asset that people would use, day in and day
out, and they would pay you a lot for it. And if you could build this and get a
runway 10 or 15 miles close to San Diego Airport, you could make a billion
dollars from doing that.
Here’s another one. Here’s a big idea. If you could do a genetic test that covers a
thousand disease conditions, drug interactions, inherited disease potentials, and
fitness, nutrition and exercise results for $350, you could provide that for $350,
you’d be saving people $150,000 and giving them a chance to live better lives
than they might otherwise. You could, if you could so that, make a billion
dollars.
I’ll give you one more. Here’s a bid idea. If you could deploy a semiconductor
that solves the indoor reception problem on cell phones, right? So all over the
world people walk into buildings, pick up their cell phone, and they lose
reception, but many of those people have no wired lines and they have no other
way to get the Internet. If you could build a semiconductor to solve that problem,
my God, you would have a billion-dollar opportunity.
And these are all deals that I have crafted narratives for. You’ll hear about them
in Pitch Mastery, throughout it. But these are big. I tell big stories, big
narratives, not small ones, right? Now you start to get characters, both business
characters and human characters people care about.
The next thing is, the way I do it is I deepen the problem. I deepen the problem.
People spend too little time on the problem. Deepen the problem. I’ll give you a
fun one I just thought of in my mind as it started spinning up and want to help you
guys understand this.
So here’s a problem, right? Everybody loves Chinese food, loves Chinese food.
But the only place you can get good Chinese food is at P.F. Chang’s or in a
restaurant. If you try and cook it at home, it doesn’t come out right, doesn’t keep
the wok hot enough. Home stoves won’t get hot enough to cook Chinese food to
give it that amazing flavor and fresh wok’d whatever-ness. And it’s important,
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 7 of 17 --

T: Tell the Story
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved. 	8
whatever-ness, right? So this is a massive problem because people all over the
country want to eat Chinese food a lot more than they do, but the only way they
can do that is to spend a lot of money by going out. It’s too damn hard to cook at
home because you can’t get the stove hot enough. Serious problem.
No matter how hard you try, it’s a heat problem, it’s not a trying-hard problem,
it’s not a skill problem, it’s not a get-the-right-ingredients problem, it’s not a
training problem. Just can’t get the wok hot enough at home. It’s massive. You
know how many people want to eat and cook and enjoy Chinese food at home.
They can’t, they can’t. So the way I do it is I deepen the problem. Really, really
explore it from many angles.
Then the next thing—and I believe this is unique to me, I’ve never seen anybody
do it properly. I invented this to solve a specific problem, the way I build
business narratives. But what I do is, I admit that other people have solutions to
the problem. It’s just not practical that I’m the only person that’s come up with it.
So for the wok, right? You know, there’s other ways to really get this Chinese
food at home. You could order out, have it brought in. You could cook one little
piece at a time and get enough heat on one piece. Might take you a long time. Or
you could just eat crappy cooked food and pretend that it’s good. I mean, there’s
ways that you could solve this.
I just think our wok heat accelerator is a better solution, better than anything else
out there, but there are ways to solve this today. We’re not the only people in the
world that have thought about this. Then, when I do it, I will then move into,
“Before I tell you my solution—I alluded to it, the wok heat super hotness
accelerator—we need to understand that this didn’t just come about as a flash of
brilliance, right? The gods didn’t say it’s time for Oren to get a great idea.
Phooph! Oh, my God, the wok heat accelerator thing!”
That’s not how it works, right? What happened is I came upon this idea because
there were forces in the market changing. The success of P.F. Chang’s had
changed the appetite of America for Chinese food, right? And the integration—
my dad was a sociologist, alright? The melting pot. There’s going to be no more
Jews or atheists or evangelist Christians. It’s all going to meld together. There’s
not going to be, you know, in a hundred years from now, the intermarriage rate—
except maybe the Amish, I don’t know—but the intermarriage rate is too damn
high. We’re all just going to integrate.
And so the taste for this Chinese food has just become cultural. It’s not related to
an Asian heritage, it’s an American cultural taste. And the ingredients are
available in every supermarket, and they’re promoted. And it wasn’t this way two
or three years ago. These forces are changing the market, and they’re building to
a crescendo, and now my business is going to take advantage of this opening
market window, super heat wok power 3000 BTU accelerator device.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 8 of 17 --

T: Tell the Story
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved. 	9
So now, that’s how I do it. I can get pretty passionate about it. It’s a little
different, very different. Remember, a business narrative is not what has
happened. It’s about what’s going to happen. People don’t want to hear about the
past in a business meeting. You need to reflect on it and show how things are
changing from the past and how that carries to the present and how a market
window is opening.
A business narrative moves from the past to the present and into the future. And
it combines—and this is the magic right here—it combines the person or the
people you’re trying to influence or sell to, to get them to buy your stuff. It
combines them with your narrative out of the past, into the present and into the
future. And I call this a future-action projection. You’re projecting them into the
story or the narrative that you have built.
And again, if you’re furiously taking notes, don’t worry. We’re going to go
through this. There’s eight steps, and we’re going to do each of those eight steps
in detail, and you will be able to do this yourself. As I’ve said, my narratives—
and soon, yours—will take people from the past through the present into an
exciting future. Businesses do this anyway. You know about a pro forma?
Everyone says, “I want to see your pro forma.” Your forward-looking projection.
You have to tell people what things are going to be like in the future. It has to be
framed. Nobody wants to know, “Here’s what we did last summer,” unless they
can know, “Here’s what’s going to happen next summer.” The past has to serve a
purpose. A narrative, a story, has to serve a purpose to frame what the future is
going to be like. Not through analysis and paradigmatic thinking, but through
narrative.
When you think about the opposite of giving a narrative about the future—“Last
year, we did a million dollars in revenue, and things are changing dramatically.
And next year, let me tell you this, I have no idea what’s going to happen.” If you
say this, people will abandon you, they’ll sell off your stock and they’ll quit your
company and they won’t do business with you.
So the narrative has to build on the past, introduce characters that people care
about, characters that are doing big things that are important and that people cheer
for and want to succeed. And those characters are harnessing changes, and they
understand those changes, and they can provide insight into those changes, and
then they can bring you into that changing world and make you part of it. That’s
the job of a business narrative.
Now that we have gotten super-excited about the reasons to tell stories and some
of the process, let’s go through, as I suggested earlier, the eight steps.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 9 of 17 --

T: Tell the Story
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved. 	10
Step One: This should be no surprise. Build a damn character that people care
about. A character that people care about. And you can only do that with the big
idea, because when you’re telling a business narrative, there are really two
characters. There’s you and the business, you and the business or the product.
The business or the product is important, but it’s not a human being, so you’ve
got to build yourself as the character people can emote with at the right time.
Let me think about this. The fact you went to Stanford, you sold a technology,
you were best friends with Arnold Schwarzenegger, and you know the business
manager for Barack Obama. It’s not compelling. It’s interesting, but it’s not
compelling to the business. So what is compelling? We said it before. A
compelling character is undertaking something big with real consequences and
stakes. And that character is marshalling forces of change to render something
with extremely high value and will have an important result in the lives of real
people and that’s understandable.
Hold on a second. You cannot walk in a room and say, “I’m a compelling
character undertaking this big thing with real consequences and stakes and
marshalling forces of change, and I’m making a difference in the lives of real
people.” You can’t just walk in and say that. You have to show it, and you have
to demonstrate that. And the way you set the stage, the way you set the stage to
do this is to talk about your big idea.
Step Two: Talk about the problem. Really deepen the problem, deepen the
problem. As I said, in the market for woks, no home stove from Rancho Santa Fe
to Brooklyn, can get a damn wok hot enough to cook great Chinese food. And
millions of people want to enjoy delicious Chinese food that is well-prepared,
myself included, but they cannot because of this technology failure.
Who invented this stuff? Jack Welch at GE? If you see this, come see me. How
the he—do you build a stove that doesn’t get hot enough to cook food? People
don’t want to eat out every time because it’s expensive, it’s a real problem. And
it prevails through the entire culture of the United States. Deep problem.
Think about it this way. You want to land at San Diego Airport in a private jet?
Good luck! Prepare your checkbook and set your watch back an hour because
you’re about to get hosed. They may not let you land because they want to land a
jet with 170 people in it. They’re going to send you an hour north to Palomar,
okay? It’s a real problem. If you’re in business, you’ve got to get to your
meeting.
Or think about it this way. It’s January fifth. You’re in Kansas enjoying a movie
on your cell phone of your brother doing a backward flip, a three-rail ollie to the
off-camera down slide in 40-inch powder in Whistler Village. Are you enjoying
this? And you’re freezing you’re a—off because you’re standing outside. Nearly
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 10 of 17 --

T: Tell the Story
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved. 	11
every building in North America and in the world has the indoor connection
problem. Modern cell phone semiconductors don’t do well in houses, apartments,
and office buildings. Huge problem, deep problem.
Hey, are you at risk for any disease? Cancer? Asthma? Seizures due to adverse
reaction to common drugs? Well, then be prepared to spend $!50,000 with 20
different companies that can help you to find out which wrecking ball the grim
reaper has launched in your general direction. Bombs away! Right? Deep
problem. Deepen the problem.
Step Three: Introduce the character. Introduce the character, right? The human
character—yourself. So once you deepen the problem, then you can introduce
and say, “This is something I’m involved with because of this reason. Here’s my
relationship to the problem.” And I’ll give you an example that’s deeply
personal. You know, I got involved with this genetics company. I suffered a
personal family loss that was maybe preventable, so I wanted to rise myself up
and solve the problem everyone has of losing important family members, when
maybe it could be prevented.
Oh, I know, we’ll have to raise tens, maybe millions of dollars, maybe a hundred
million dollars, right? And yet the science is still at a beginning stage, and I know
we face major obstacles in this company getting customers and having the
revenues and getting the science all to work. But I am making this change
happen, I’m part of it, and I’m inviting other visionaries and partners in this grand
adventure to join me because it’s important.
That’s my relationship with this problem. A character you care about because I’m
going to do something great, weld myself to the problem. You’ll be doing the
same.
Step Four: You’ve got to stay away from the abstract. If you get into it, you’ve
got to bring yourself back. Buyers live in the real world with real stakes, and you
need to anchor your pitch and your business narrative in reality, so it has a core, a
core that is tied to the earth in a human way. Tied to the earth, because people
understand what happens on the earth and in the physical world. Big animal
pictures, human time scales, physical reality, actual physical things working
together.
There’s two great ways to anchor your ideas or your product or your notions or
your pitch or what you’re selling. Two great ways to anchor them, and one is,
provide and admit there are other solutions to the problem. You know what?
George Foreman actually has a pretty good wok. It’s made in China, gets pretty
hot, and if I had no other options, the super-conducting Mega 2000 whatever-it-
was I invented—if a meteor landed and blew that thing up, I would actually buy
the George Foreman. It does a decent job. It’s not great, it’s not great. No one is
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 11 of 17 --

T: Tell the Story
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved. 	12
going to trust and believe you if you say, “I’m a Lone Ranger. I’ve single-
handedly discovered this problem, and I’m the only one with an answer to it.” So
you can’t act blind to other solutions. You have to admit.
I was, in my early years, I was an analyst and I went to Cal Tech and JPL, Jet
Propulsion Labs, and Lawrence Livermore Laboratories, and UCLA—the
deepest, darkest parts of the basement—to look at technology. And I would meet
with inventors and scientists who would be working at the one-micron level,
never before achieved, of a microchip.
Or looking into space with deep-space camera, a low-light camera, so amazing
you can’t imagine that anybody else in the world would ever have thought about
it. A thousand monkeys at a thousand typewriters for a thousand years would not
again think up this idea to have a low-light camera that can see so far into space,
you actually see the back of your head by looking forward.
That’s what I thought. And all of a sudden, I find out there’s a friggin’
conference on the thing twice a year in Czechoslovakia. Nobody comes up with
something so discrete and original that there’s not another version of it. You have
to admit about those other versions.
Another way to anchor it is once you find yourself getting abstract, tell a more
traditional story, the thing I’ve scared you away from about the problem. Tell it
in human terms, time scales, and actions. I’ll give you a quick example. You
know, this wok thing we’re talking about—“Yeah, we’re going to make a hundred
million dollars because it’s going to be on infomercials and it’s going to be in
Sears and it’s going to be in Target and might be in almost every family home.”
You know what? The owners of some of the largest stove companies in the world
are going to even own this product.
But I want to tell you something. It’s not just about making money and all these
abstract terms. I’ll tell you about a family, Mr. and Mrs. Jones. You know,
they’re on the brink of divorce. They’re fighting. And finally Mrs. Jones says,
“I’m going to make you a meal, and we’re going to sit down and we’re going to
enjoy it, and it’s going to be delicious. And it’s going to be part of your heritage,
and it’s going to be like a meal that you ate when you were a kid.” And Mr. Jones
says, “You know, this might be the thing that brings us together.”
And she gets the recipe, and she dutifully measures out the sale to the
micromilligram and all the ingredients, and cuts the chicken breast just so high
and cooks it on the stove, and the soy sauce is put in at exactly the right moment.
And she’s studied on the Internet, the Interwebs, this recipe, and researched all the
recipes, and this is the best one. And she prepares this food and it looks beautiful,
and she serves it. And Mr. Jones looks down and he says, “Crap. Tastes
terrible.” And they get divorced. Okay? This affects real people, right? My
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 12 of 17 --

T: Tell the Story
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved. 	13
product, the super heat wok stove home thing accelerator 3000 is about real
people, it’s not just about making money. It affects human lives.
Well, this is why the business narrative from the personal story. Because the hero
is a product or service, but the people don’t care about products or services. They
care about you and they care about humans, so remember that as you build this in
Step Four.
Now I’ll move on to Step Five. Because it’s a business narrative, we can’t ignore
the value proposition. We’ll just do that using the idea introduction pattern that’s
in the book. Very easy. Four target customers who are dissatisfied with the
current offerings in the market. My idea and my product is a new kind of
category, and it provides the following solution to the following problems and has
the following features. And unlike all these crappy competing products, my
product has feature one and feature two and feature three and feature four.
We talked about this before. I’ll give you the same examples so you can hear it
again. For companies with large buildings in California and in Arizona who are
dissatisfied with their aging solar panels, my product is a plug-and-play solar
accelerator that provides 35 percent more energy from old existing panels. And
unlike the cost of replacing old panels, my product is inexpensive and, guess
what, it has no moving parts. Great. Thirty-five percent savings, has no moving
parts, and use your existing infrastructure.
I’ll give you another one. The value proposition. I sell computer monitors and
workstations. For busy executives who don’t have enough workspace on their
computer monitor, my product is a visual array that provides flat-screen monitors
linked together, as many as 12. They can fit on any desk, and unlike the common
do-it-yourself solutions that have just two or three monitors haphazardly set up,
my visual array lets executives use Excel, Firefox, Gmail, Skype, PhotoShop,
InDesign, and even had a trading desk open at the same time, with no confusing
windows to sort through. It’s a value proposition.
Remember, these are about numbers and abstract things. We can all get very lost
in the numbers. You risk triggering what is the paradigmatic mode, and that is
obviously the analytical mode, when faced with too much financial data. So then
we have to go into Step Six.
Step Six: Bring it back to big animal pictures, human scale, human time frames,
human stories. If the numbers are exciting and compelling and the economic case
is really interesting and you can see how the numbers tie together, you still have
to communicate how this affects real people. You do that very easily by saying,
you say, “This stuff affects real lives, and we see it every day in our business.
Even the paper and the copier business. We affect real lives, we don’t just sell
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 13 of 17 --

T: Tell the Story
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved. 	14
paper and copiers and make money and go home and buy BMWs and travel to
Hawaii once a year. We affect lives, like we’ve said before.”
Think about this. If you’re going to give a presentation to sell your company to
the board that’s making the decision on what price to pay, or you’re going into
Walmart the next day and proposing they buy a million of your widgets, and the
night before your copier jams. Your paper jams and the copier jammed, and it’s a
mess. And then you’ve got a real problem. You’ve got to go to FedEx Kinko’s,
and they print it wrong, and you go into the meeting tired. And you forgot your
narrative, right? Copiers make a big difference. Don’t count them out. They
affect human lives.
So once you give the economics and you make the financial picture or the buying
value proposition real, you do have to bring it back. Big animal pictures. Big
animal pictures.
Step Seven: What’s going to happen that is good to the person you are pitching or
selling or influencing? I call this a future-action projection. We have talked
about the past in our narrative. We have taken them into the current and talked
about what’s happening. The three moving forces. We covered it in framing, but
we might as well do it here.
When you describe your idea, your project, your product, whatever it is, you first
give it context by framing it against market forces and trending patterns. So if
you want to talk to somebody about what’s going to happen to them in the future,
you have to explain how the present is changing. And you can conceptualize in
terms of economic forces what has changed financially in the market for the
product you sell.
Is credit more available, is financial optimism higher, or there’s been increases
and decreases in buying rates or interest rates or inflation, the value of the dollar,
or whatever? These are all examples of economic forces. And then you can talk
about the trending of social forces, as I did in the wok example. You highlight
what emerging social changes there are in people’s behavior, what patterns exist
to make your idea fit in the movement of people’s desires.
An obvious example is the market for cars. So concern over the environment is a
social force, and that’s driving people to get these Priuses and these Leafs and
whatever these things are that get lots of mileage and protect the environment.
Another force, another driving force is technology forces—like technological
forces can flatten an entire existing business model and flatten entire industries.
The demand shifts from one product to another. Look at Amazon. Just ask
Borders if technology can have any impact on economics. They’re gone. Poof!
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 14 of 17 --

T: Tell the Story
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved. 	15
Amazon. And electronics. And Internet change is rapid and constant. You can
talk about these changes. So that’s how the past moves into the present.
Then you’re going to add the following. “Here’s where I am within those
changes. Here’s where I am within those changes and how I play with the social
and economic and technological changes. And here’s what’s going to happen
next.” This is the amazing part. And if there’s one thing you’ve really got to pay
attention to, at this step, is the future-action projection—you integrate them into
the story. Right? They become part of the story.
You talked about what happened in the past and you came into being, all the
moving forces, and now where it’s going. Where it’s going. The market window
open. You are the buyer of woks, my woks. You’re the buyer of my genetic
tests, right? And you’re going to have this amazing financial future by getting
involved with me, because I’m in control of these forces. I’m in control of these
forces, and I’m in an amazing narrative that you want to be part of. So you’re
going to paint the picture of the future they’re going to have with you.
And there’s a last step to doing this. You’re going to add—Step Eight: You’re
going to add dramatic tension. So this is not like a movie, right? You cannot say
to the investor what happened in the past. “Last quarter, our product orders were
through the roof. But then one of our engineers quit, and, oh my God, our
company went into enormous turmoil. We didn’t know what to do. We all got
together around the fireside and came up with this great idea and deployed the
great idea, and guess what? It worked! It worked! And we were covered. And
now we’re selling more than ever.”
That’s a terrible story, because it gives you low status. Yes, you did something
great. Terrible narrative. It gives you low status. You don’t tell people in the
eleventh hour, like in a movie, that you succeeded and pulled it out and did good
things. That doesn’t serve your business narrative, because people are not going
to pay you just for a description of this rollercoaster ride. They’re going to pay
you for certainty, for your reliability.
A lot of people go to McDonald’s all over the world because they know exactly
what they’re going to get—certainty. So dramatic tension doesn’t come from
telling wild story about how you had pains and failures and successes and had a
rollercoaster ride. It comes from something totally different. Dramatic tension
comes from pushing back on the other person and saying, “Are you good enough
to be part of my story? Are you good enough to be part of the future that we’re
building? Are you good enough to part of this narrative?” Because these changes
that I’ve described to you—social, economic, technological—there’s a window of
opportunity that is opening.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 15 of 17 --

T: Tell the Story
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved. 	16
And just like in “The Matrix,” you now face a decision point. You can take the
red pill or the blue pill. If you take the blue pill, then you are doing the right
thing. You’ll be joining us. You’ll be making good things happen for the
environment, for the business. You’ll be having fun. You’ll be joining our ranks,
and you’ll be in on the grand adventure. And you and I will seek to overthrow
these suckey problems that we’ve talked about today, and we will do something
important, and life will be good and easy. And we’ll be friends, and you’ll make
money, and the clouds will part, and the weather will warm up. Your ailments
will be cured. I’m not sure we have to go that far, but take the blue pill
If you take the red pill, then you go on your own damn adventure, and you watch
the rest of this story unfold with me at the center of it, making money and having
fun. You’ll watch it from the sidelines. So how do you say this in business
terms? I think you do it like this.
“We have a limited number of customers, and I have a huge value that I can
deliver. Limited number of customers. We can’t take everybody on, can’t sell to
everybody. I deliver huge value to my customers, and I can only work with
customers who do things the right way. So I’d like to hear from you right now.
How do you see us working together? Do you do things the right way?”
If you do everything else right, one, two, three, four, five, six, seven—when you
get to Step Eight, you can do this. You cannot walk in the room without doing
Steps One through Seven and say, “Hey, you bastards, you want to join me? I
wonder, do you do things the right way? I can only take on so many customers.
I’m not sure if you’re going to be the right one.” Can’t do that. That’s Step One,
not Step Eight.
So when you do this, it forces your audience to understand that they have
something to lose, that you might go into this future that you’ve painted, without
them, and it makes them pay attention. They become invested because now, after
giving them all this value, this insight, this understanding, this wonderful
narrative, just being in your presence as you tell this magnificent story, you are
going to subtly remove it, withdraw it. And that instinctively is going to make
people reach for it. Remember, we chase that which moves away from us. We
want that which we cannot have, and we only value those things that we work for.
So those are the eight steps. I’ll give you an example of this being done exactly
the way I do it in another video, and there will also be a worksheet to take you
through this specifically.
Just a quick review. The steps are: Build a character people actually care about.
Deepen the problem, don’t describe the problem and then rush and explain how
you solved it. Deepen the problem. Grrrr, you know, make it deeply felt.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 16 of 17 --

T: Tell the Story
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved. 	17
Introduce characters who have high stakes in things to win and lose. Yourself as
a character, how did you become involved with this deep problem and the
product?
Anchor all of this, Step Four, anchor all of this in reality. You can’t disappear
into this abstract discussion of financing and what the product does and features.
Step Five, deliver the value proposition in clear and certain terms. Do not screw
this up or I’ll come get you. It’s not hard to do, alright? It’s not hard to do, Step
Five.
Step Six, after you’ve talked about the features and the benefits, bring it down to
human scale. Give it a truthfulness.
Step Seven, build this future-action projection. The future is going to look like
this, and I’m going into it.
I might go in without you. And that’s Step Eight. Wrap it all up in dramatic
tension. You can do that by asking the customer what kind of customer he is and
can he play on this field of play that you’re on with the pros. You’ll start to move
away a little bit, and we’re going to talk about that next in the R in the STRONG
method. We’re going to talk about Revealing the Intrigue and moving back and
moving away and having them chase you. But you’re not going to be able to
reveal the entry until you do this right, telling the story.
Remember, and this is my final point. It’s not telling a story, it’s telling the story.
So we’ll move on then to the next video in a few, and I’ll see you when we talk
about R, Revealing the Intrigue.
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 17 of 17 --
